title: Sea salt cold-process soap
subtitle: A firm, mineral-rich bar with sea salt at 50 to 80% of oil weight and a 20% superfat
excerpt: Salt bars are dense, hard, and almost lotiony on the skin. The high coconut oil base provides the lather that salt otherwise suppresses, and the elevated superfat compensates for the stripping effect of sodium chloride. Cut within 30 minutes of pouring or the bar becomes almost impossible to slice.
sourceNotes: Salt bar formulation based on Soap Queen and Brambleberry salt bar guides. High coconut oil and elevated superfat are standard practice for this bar type.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter (~8 to 10 individual bars after cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Rerun through a lye calculator with 20% superfat setting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Salt bars harden very quickly and must be cut within 30 minutes of pouring. Cure for 4 weeks; they improve to a very hard, polished bar over time.
recipe.temperatureNote: Combine oils and lye solution at 45 to 50 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years from cure date. Salt bars are very stable. Discard if rancid.

recipeTools.notes: Individual cavity moulds are easier as you skip the cutting step
For cutting within 30 minutes of pouring

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oils left unsaponified in a soap formula. Salt bars use a high superfat of 20% because sodium chloride has a slightly stripping effect on skin; the free oils restore moisture.

A cold-process soap containing a large proportion of sodium chloride (sea salt or Dead Sea salt) added at trace. Salt causes the bar to harden extremely quickly and produces a dense, polished bar with a lotion-like feel when wet.
The emulsification point in cold-process soap making when the batter holds a visible drizzle on the surface. Salt is added at light trace.
A saturated fatty acid high in coconut oil. High-lauric formulas are used in salt bars because salt suppresses lather; the abundant lauric acid compensates.
The 4 to 8 week resting period after unmoulding cold-process soap. Salt bars cure to an exceptionally hard, smooth finish.

content.text: is one of the most unusual cold-process bars to make and to use. Lathered up it feels soft and almost creamy against the skin, quite unlike its rock-hard appearance would suggest. The formula relies heavily on
from the 80% coconut oil base to maintain lather in the presence of salt, and a 20%

to counteract salt's drying tendency. Timing is everything: salt bars harden within 30 minutes of pouring and must be cut before they set, or not at all. Individual cavity moulds sidestep this entirely. The
produces a very hard, smooth, almost polished bar. Shelf life is 2 years.
technique before starting. Wear protective goggles and gloves throughout. Have your mould ready before you start the lye solution. There is no time to find equipment once the batter is at trace.

items.prepNote: 80% of oil weight; high lauric acid base
Calculated at 20% superfat; confirm with lye calculator
50% of oil weight; up to 80% for a denser bar

content.text: technique. Melt coconut oil and combine with shea butter and castor oil. Prepare the lye solution. Allow both to reach 45 to 50 degrees Celsius. Combine lye and oils and stick-blend to light

Add the sea salt all at once and stir by hand to combine. Work quickly: the batter will thicken rapidly once salt is added. Pour immediately into the mould. If using a loaf mould, cut into bars within 30 minutes. If using individual cavity moulds, leave to set for 24 hours before unmoulding. Cure on an open rack for 4 weeks.

Cured bars keep for 2 years. The high saturated fat content makes them very stable. Store in a cool, dry place. Discard if the bar smells rancid. Salt bars do not show DOS as readily as less saturated formulas.
